abstract: This article examines the transformative influence of the Circle of Concerned African Women Theologians in reshaping the landscape of higher theological education across the continent. Rooted in the principles of African Women’s Theology, the study investigates the organization's efforts to champion gender equality, inclusivity, and intersectional solidarity within the historically male-dominated theological academy. By tracing the Circle’s historical trajectory, the research highlights its success in integrating gender-sensitive curricula and mentoring a new generation of African women scholars. Utilizing a qualitative research design and adopting African Women’s Theology as an interpretive lens, this paper underscores the significant strides made by the Circle in engendering African theological discourse and challenging the structural barriers within religious higher education.
